lessons.title: Why Simplify, and What a K-Map Is
lessons.content:
Imagine you have a logic circuit built from gates (the tiny parts that compute AND, OR, and NOT). Often the first version you write is bigger than it needs to be. **Minimizing** means finding a simpler way to compute the *exact same* output using **fewer and smaller gates**. Fewer gates means a circuit that is cheaper to build, faster to react, and uses less power. So simplifying logic is not just tidiness, it saves real money and energy.

You already know about **truth tables** (a list of every input combination and the output it should give) and **Boolean algebra** (the rules for AND, OR, NOT). You *can* simplify by juggling algebra rules, but that is fiddly and easy to get wrong.

A **Karnaugh map** (K-map for short, named after Maurice Karnaugh) is a smarter trick: it is just a truth table re-drawn as a **grid**. The grid is arranged so that simplifying becomes **visual**. You look for groups of adjacent 1s, draw a loop around each group, and each loop turns into one simpler term. It is a bit like spotting a pattern by grouping similar things together on a board.

Recap: a K-map is a truth table laid out as a grid so you can simplify logic by *eye* instead of by algebra. The payoff is smaller, cheaper, faster circuits. The next lessons show how the grid is laid out and how the grouping works.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'minimize' a logic circuit?
choices: Find a simpler circuit that produces the exact same output using fewer and smaller gates
Make the circuit produce a different, easier output
Shrink the physical size of each individual transistor
Reduce the number of inputs the circuit is allowed to have
items.explanation: Minimizing keeps the behaviour (the truth table) identical while using fewer/smaller gates. It does not change what the circuit computes, and it is not about transistor size or removing inputs.

items.prompt: Why is using fewer gates a good thing?
choices: Fewer gates generally means a cheaper, faster, lower-power circuit
Fewer gates make the circuit look more complicated
Fewer gates always change the output for the better
Fewer gates require a larger power supply
items.explanation: Each gate costs money, adds delay, and draws power. Trimming gates saves all three. The output stays the same; that is the whole point of minimizing.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a Karnaugh map?
choices: A truth table re-drawn as a grid so that logic can be simplified visually
A list of every gate available in a chip
A graph of voltage over time
A circuit that automatically rewires itself
items.explanation: A K-map holds exactly the same information as a truth table, just arranged as a grid. The grid layout is what makes simplification something you can see by eye.

items.prompt: On a K-map, how do you actually do the simplifying?
choices: Circle groups of adjacent 1s; each group becomes one simpler term
Erase every 1 and keep only the 0s
Add up the decimal values of all the cells
Rotate the grid until the 1s line up diagonally
items.explanation: Grouping adjacent 1s and reading each loop as a single term is the core K-map technique. The remaining lessons build up the rules for doing this correctly.

items.prompt: What background does a K-map assume you already have?
choices: Truth tables and basic Boolean algebra (AND, OR, NOT)
Calculus and differential equations
How to program a microcontroller in C
How to solder a circuit board
items.explanation: A K-map is just a re-arranged truth table simplified with Boolean ideas, so truth tables and AND/OR/NOT are the only prerequisites. No higher math or hardware skills are needed.

items.prompt: Compared with simplifying by Boolean algebra alone, the K-map's main advantage is that it is:
choices: Visual, so it is easier and less error-prone for small problems
The only method that gives a correct answer
Able to handle thousands of variables by hand
Faster only because it ignores some of the inputs
items.explanation: Algebra works but is fiddly; the K-map turns the same job into spotting visual groups, which is easier to get right for small circuits. It does not ignore inputs, and it does not scale to huge problems (later lessons cover that limit).

items.prompt: A K-map and the matching truth table contain:
choices: The same information, just arranged differently
Completely different information
Only the inputs, never the outputs
Only the rows where the output is 0
items.explanation: The K-map is simply a re-layout of the truth table; no information is added or removed. Both list the output for every input combination.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best captures what K-map grouping feels like?
choices: Spotting a pattern by grouping similar items together on a grid
Sorting a list of numbers from smallest to largest
Measuring the length of a wire with a ruler
Counting how many times a light blinks
items.explanation: Grouping adjacent 1s on a grid is a pattern-spotting activity, much like clustering similar things on a board. That intuition is exactly what the K-map turns into a method.

lessons.title: The Grid Layout: Gray-Code Adjacency
lessons.content:
Here is the clever part of a K-map: the cells are arranged so that any two cells **right next to each other differ in exactly one input variable**. That single-variable difference is what lets two cells merge into one simpler term, so the whole grid is built to make 'next to each other' mean 'almost identical'.

To get that, we do **not** label the axes in plain counting order ($00, 01, 10, 11$). Notice that going from $01$ to $10$ flips *two* bits at once. Instead we use **Gray code**: $00, 01, 11, 10$. Read across that sequence and only **one bit changes at each step** (00 to 01 flips the last bit, 01 to 11 flips the first, 11 to 10 flips the last). Gray code is just an ordering where neighbours differ by a single bit.

The smallest map has 2 variables and is a **2 x 2 grid (4 cells)**. A 3-variable map is a **2 x 4 grid (8 cells)**: one variable picks the row, and two Gray-coded variables pick the column. In general an $n$-variable map has $2^n$ cells, one per row of the truth table. Each cell holds the output (a 0 or a 1) for that input combination.

One more rule that surprises people: the edges **wrap around**. The leftmost column is treated as next to the rightmost column, and the top row as next to the bottom row, because in Gray code those also differ by one bit.

Recap: Gray-code labels make neighbours differ by one variable, and the edges wrap. Get this layout solid; every later step rides on it.

items.prompt: What is special about two cells that sit right next to each other on a K-map?
choices: They differ in exactly one input variable
They always hold the same output value
Their decimal minterm numbers are always consecutive
They differ in exactly two input variables
items.explanation: The grid is built so adjacency means a one-variable difference. That single-bit change is precisely what lets two neighbouring cells combine into a simpler term.

items.prompt: Which sequence is Gray code, used to label a 4-position K-map axis?
items.explanation: Gray code is 00, 01, 11, 10: each step flips just one bit, including the wrap from 10 back to 00. Plain binary (00,01,10,11) flips two bits between 01 and 10, which would break adjacency.

items.prompt: Why are the axes NOT labelled in plain binary counting order (00, 01, 10, 11)?
choices: Because the 01-to-10 step flips two bits, so neighbours would differ in two variables and could not be merged
Because plain binary cannot represent the number 2
Because it would need twice as many cells
Because the output values would change
items.explanation: Going 01 to 10 changes both bits at once. If neighbours differed in two variables, looping them would not match the rule that lets cells merge. Gray code keeps every neighbour one bit apart.

items.prompt: How many cells does an n-variable K-map have, and what does each cell hold?
choices: 2^n cells, each holding the output for one input combination
n cells, each holding one input variable
2n cells, each holding a gate
n^2 cells, each holding a Gray-code label
items.explanation: There is one cell per truth-table row, and a truth table for n variables has 2^n rows. Each cell stores that row's output (a 0 or 1).

items.prompt: What shape is a standard 2-variable K-map?
choices: A 2 x 2 grid (4 cells)
A 2 x 4 grid (8 cells)
A 4 x 4 grid (16 cells)
A single row of 4 cells
items.explanation: Two variables give 2^2 = 4 minterms, drawn as a 2-by-2 grid. The 2x4 (8 cells) is the 3-variable map and 4x4 (16 cells) is the 4-variable map.

items.prompt: A 3-variable K-map is usually drawn as which grid?
choices: A 2 x 4 grid (8 cells): one variable on the row, two Gray-coded on the columns
A 3 x 3 grid (9 cells)
A 4 x 4 grid (16 cells)
A 1 x 8 single row
items.explanation: Three variables give 2^3 = 8 cells. The usual layout puts one variable on two rows and two Gray-coded variables across four columns.

items.prompt: What does 'wrap-around' adjacency mean on a K-map?
choices: The left edge is treated as next to the right edge, and the top as next to the bottom
You may only group cells that touch in the middle of the grid
The grid must be folded into a physical tube before use
Corner cells can never be grouped with anything
items.explanation: In Gray code the first and last labels also differ by one bit, so the outer edges are neighbours. The leftmost and rightmost columns wrap together, as do the top and bottom rows.

items.prompt: You fill a K-map from a function written as a list of minterms (the input combinations where the output is 1). Where do the 1s go?
choices: In the cells whose minterm numbers appear in that list; all other cells get 0
In every cell, then you erase the wrong ones at random
Only in the four corner cells
In the cells whose numbers are even
items.explanation: A minterm list names exactly the input combinations that give output 1. Place a 1 in each listed cell and 0 everywhere else; that is the K-map of the function.

items.prompt: On a 3-variable map with columns labelled 00, 01, 11, 10, which two columns are neighbours by wrap-around?
choices: The 00 column and the 10 column (the far-left and far-right)
The 01 column and the 11 column
No columns wrap on a 3-variable map
Every column is a neighbour of every other column
items.explanation: 00 and 10 differ in just one bit, so the far-left and far-right columns are adjacent even though they sit at opposite ends of the grid. That is wrap-around adjacency.

lessons.title: Grouping Rules and 4-Variable Maps
lessons.content:
Now we put the layout to work. The 4-variable map is a **4 x 4 grid (16 cells)**, with two Gray-coded variables on the rows and two on the columns. It is big enough to show every grouping rule.

To simplify, you cover all the 1s with loops, following these rules:

- A loop (a **group**) must contain only 1s.
- A group's size must be a **power of two**: 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 cells. Never 3, 5, 6, and so on.
- A group must be a rectangle (a straight block), and it may **wrap around** the edges.
- **Make every group as big as legally possible**, and use as few groups as you can.

Why do bigger groups win? Each time a group **doubles** in size, it removes one more variable from that term. Concretely, in an $n$-variable map a group of $2^k$ cells eliminates $k$ variables, leaving a product term with $n-k$ letters. A group of 1 keeps all $n$ letters; a group of 16 covers the whole map and the term disappears entirely (the output is always 1). So a bigger loop equals a shorter, cheaper term. That is why the guiding habit is **largest group first**.

A single isolated 1 (no neighbouring 1s) still must be covered, as a group of size 1.

Recap: cover all the 1s with the fewest, largest power-of-two rectangles, wrapping allowed. Bigger groups drop more variables, giving simpler terms.

items.prompt: Which group size is NOT allowed on a K-map?
choices: A group of 3 cells
A group of 2 cells
A group of 4 cells
A group of 8 cells
items.explanation: Group sizes must be powers of two: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. Three is not a power of two, so a group of 3 is illegal even if three 1s sit in a row.

items.prompt: On a 4-variable map, a legal group of 4 cells leaves a product term with how many letters?
choices: 2 letters
4 letters
1 letter
0 letters
items.explanation: A group of 4 = 2^2 cells eliminates k=2 variables. In a 4-variable map that leaves 4 - 2 = 2 letters in the term.

items.prompt: Why do we always try to make each group as large as possible?
choices: A larger group removes more variables, giving a shorter and cheaper term
A larger group is easier to draw neatly
A larger group changes the output to all 1s
Smaller groups are illegal in every K-map
items.explanation: Each doubling of a group drops one more variable. Bigger loops therefore yield terms with fewer letters, which means fewer/simpler gates. That is the reason for the 'largest group first' habit.

items.prompt: A group of 8 cells in a 4-variable map leaves a term with how many letters?
choices: 1 letter
3 letters
0 letters
8 letters
items.explanation: Eight cells = 2^3, so k = 3 variables are eliminated. In a 4-variable map that leaves 4 - 3 = 1 letter. Bigger group, shorter term.

items.prompt: A lone 1 sits with 0s on all four sides. What must you do with it?
choices: Cover it as a group of size 1, which keeps all four variables in that term
Ignore it to save gates
Erase it because it cannot be grouped
Merge it diagonally with the nearest 1
items.explanation: Every 1 must be covered. With no neighbouring 1s the largest legal group is just itself (size 1), which removes zero variables and keeps the full 4-letter term. Diagonal grouping is not allowed.

items.prompt: Can a group legally wrap around from the right edge of the map to the left edge?
choices: Yes, because the left and right edges are adjacent in Gray code
No, groups must stay inside the visible borders
Only on a 2-variable map
Only if the group has exactly 3 cells
items.explanation: Wrap-around adjacency means the outer edges are neighbours, so a group may span from one edge across to the opposite edge. The four corners of a 4-variable map, for instance, can form one group of 4.

items.prompt: Two 1s sit side by side. The group of 2 covering them eliminates how many variables?
choices: 1 variable
2 variables
0 variables
4 variables
items.explanation: A group of 2 = 2^1 cells eliminates k = 1 variable, because the two cells differ in exactly that one variable. The variable that changes across the pair drops out of the term.

items.prompt: A group covers all 16 cells of a 4-variable map. What does the term become?
choices: It vanishes; the output is simply 1 for every input
It keeps all four variables
It becomes a single-letter term
It is illegal because 16 is too big
items.explanation: A group of 16 = 2^4 eliminates all 4 variables (4 - 4 = 0 letters). The function is constantly 1. Sixteen is a power of two, so the group is perfectly legal.

items.prompt: Which is the best grouping strategy?
choices: Cover every 1 using the fewest groups, each as large as legally possible
Use as many tiny groups as you can
Cover only half the 1s and leave the rest
Cover the 0s and ignore the 1s
items.explanation: Fewest groups means fewer OR-ed terms; largest groups means fewer letters per term. Both shrink the circuit. Every 1 must be covered, and for a sum-of-products result you group the 1s, not the 0s.

lessons.title: Reading Off the Simplified Expression
lessons.content:
Once your 1s are grouped, you turn the picture back into a formula. The result is in **sum-of-products (SOP)** form: each group becomes one **product term** (variables ANDed together), and then you OR all the product terms together. 'Sum' is the OR, 'product' is the AND.

To read a single group, ask: **which variables stay constant across every cell in the loop?**

- A variable that is **1** for the whole group appears as itself (for example $A$).
- A variable that is **0** for the whole group appears **complemented**, written $\\overline{A}$ (read 'not A' or 'A-bar').
- A variable that **changes** within the group has been eliminated, so you drop it.

Example: suppose a group of four cells all share $C=1$ and $D=1$, while $A$ and $B$ each take both values. Then $C$ and $D$ stay constant (so they appear) and $A,B$ vary (so they drop). The term is $C\\cdot D$. If another group of two all share $A=1$, $B=0$, $D=0$, that term is $A\\cdot\\overline{B}\\cdot\\overline{D}$. The whole simplified function is the OR of the groups:
$$F = C\\cdot D + A\\cdot\\overline{B}\\cdot\\overline{D}.$$

Recap: each loop gives one AND term (constant-1 variables plain, constant-0 variables barred, changing variables dropped), and you OR the loops together. That OR-of-ANDs is the minimized SOP expression.

items.prompt: In a sum-of-products (SOP) expression, what do 'sum' and 'product' mean?
choices: Sum = OR of the terms; product = AND of the variables inside a term
Sum = ordinary addition; product = ordinary multiplication
Sum = AND; product = OR
Sum = NOT; product = XOR
items.explanation: In Boolean form 'product' is AND (variables ANDed within a term) and 'sum' is OR (terms ORed together). So SOP is an OR of AND-terms.

items.prompt: To read a group, you check each variable. A variable that stays 1 across the whole group appears in the term as:
choices: Itself, for example A
Its complement, A-bar
Dropped entirely
A constant 0
items.explanation: A variable held at 1 throughout the group appears uncomplemented (just A). A variable held at 0 would appear barred; a variable that changes is dropped.

items.prompt: A variable that stays 0 across the whole group appears as:
choices: Its complement, written A-bar
Itself, A
A 1
It is removed from the term
items.explanation: Held at 0 means the term is true when that variable is 0, i.e. when its complement is 1, so you write the barred form A-bar. Only variables that CHANGE get removed.

items.prompt: A variable that changes value within the group (1 in some cells, 0 in others) is:
choices: Dropped from that term
Written as itself
Written as its complement
Written twice
items.explanation: A variable that is not constant cannot help define the group, so it is eliminated. This is exactly why bigger groups (more varying variables) give shorter terms.

items.prompt: A group of 4 cells all have C = 1 and D = 1, while A and B take every combination. Circle the right group: what is its term?
choices: C AND D
A AND B
C AND D, both complemented
A AND B AND C AND D
items.explanation: C and D stay constant at 1, so they appear plain; A and B vary, so they drop. The simplified term is C AND D. Because two variables were eliminated, the group must have been size 4.

items.prompt: A group of 2 cells all have A = 1, B = 0, and D = 0, while C changes. What is the simplified term?
choices: A AND B-bar AND D-bar
A AND B AND D
A AND C
A-bar AND B AND D
items.explanation: A stays 1 (plain A); B stays 0 (B-bar); D stays 0 (D-bar); C varies and drops. The term is A AND B-bar AND D-bar. Three letters remain, matching a group of 2 in a 4-variable map.

items.prompt: You found two groups giving terms C AND D and A AND B-bar AND D-bar. How do you combine them into the final expression?
choices: OR them together: F = (C AND D) OR (A AND B-bar AND D-bar)
AND them together
Subtract the smaller term from the larger
Keep only the term with fewer letters
items.explanation: In SOP form every group is one product term and they are joined with OR. The full minimized function is the OR of all the group terms.

items.prompt: A group of two cells differs only in variable B (B is 1 in one cell, 0 in the other); both cells have A = 0. What term results?
choices: A-bar (just the single letter A-bar)
A-bar AND B
A-bar AND B-bar
B
items.explanation: A stays 0, giving A-bar; B changes, so it drops. With only those two variables involved the whole term collapses to A-bar. The single varying variable was eliminated.

items.prompt: If a group's term comes out with zero letters (everything varied), what does that tell you?
choices: The group covers the entire map, so the function is always 1
You made an error and should erase the group
The function is always 0
You must add the missing variables back in
items.explanation: Every variable varying means the group spans all 2^n cells, eliminating all variables. The output is constantly 1. (Grouping 1s can never produce a constant 0 result.)

lessons.title: Prime Implicants, Don't-Cares and Beyond
lessons.content:
A few ideas polish the method. First, vocabulary for picking groups well. A **prime implicant** is a group that is already as large as it can legally be (no bigger legal loop contains it). An **essential prime implicant** is a prime implicant that is the *only* group able to cover some particular 1. Because nothing else can cover that 1, an essential prime implicant **must** appear in the final answer. The practical recipe: pick all the essential prime implicants first, then add the fewest extra prime implicants needed to cover any leftover 1s.

Second, **don't-care cells** (written $X$). These are input combinations that can never happen, or whose output truly does not matter (for example the unused codes $1010$ to $1111$ in a 4-bit decimal digit). An $X$ is a wildcard: treat it as a $1$ **only when doing so makes a group bigger**, and as a $0$ otherwise. Used well, don't-cares often shrink the result noticeably. Never build a group whose only purpose is to cover an $X$; they are optional, never required.

Third, the limits and the tools. K-maps get unwieldy past 4 or 5 variables. The **Quine-McCluskey** method is a step-by-step table that finds the same prime implicants without a picture, which is what software can automate. Real design tools use fast minimizers (such as Espresso) on huge functions.

Finally, a hazard idea: a minimized circuit can briefly **glitch** when an input changes between two groups that merely touch. Adding one extra **redundant term** that bridges the two groups smooths the transition. It costs a gate but removes the glitch.

Recap: choose essential prime implicants first, exploit don't-cares as free wildcards, lean on Quine-McCluskey/software past a handful of variables, and add a redundant bridging term when a glitch must be prevented.

items.prompt: What is a prime implicant?
choices: A group that is already as large as it can legally be
Any single cell containing a 1
A group made only of 0s
A group of exactly three cells
items.explanation: A prime implicant is a maximal legal group: no larger legal loop contains it. Making groups as large as possible is exactly the search for prime implicants.

items.prompt: What makes a prime implicant 'essential'?
choices: It is the only group that can cover some particular 1
It is the smallest group on the map
It covers only don't-care cells
It contains the most 0s
items.explanation: If a certain 1 can be covered by just one prime implicant, that prime implicant is essential and must be in the final answer, because nothing else reaches that 1.

items.prompt: What is the recommended order for building the minimal answer?
choices: Pick all essential prime implicants first, then add the fewest extra prime implicants to cover leftover 1s
Pick the smallest groups first
Use every prime implicant on the map
Cover the don't-cares before anything else
items.explanation: Essentials are forced, so take them first; then cover whatever 1s remain with as few additional prime implicants as possible. Using all prime implicants would be wasteful and not minimal.

items.prompt: How should a don't-care (X) cell be treated when grouping?
choices: As a 1 only when it lets you form a bigger group; otherwise as a 0
Always as a 1, no matter what
Always as a 0, no matter what
Removed from the grid entirely
items.explanation: A don't-care is a wildcard. Count it as 1 when it enlarges a useful group (removing more variables); leave it as 0 otherwise. Forcing all to 1 can create needless terms.

items.prompt: Should you ever make a group whose only job is to cover a don't-care cell?
choices: No, don't-cares are optional and never need covering on their own
Yes, every don't-care must end up inside some group
Yes, but only on a 2-variable map
Only if the don't-care is in a corner
items.explanation: Don't-cares help only when they enlarge a group that is covering real 1s. A group existing solely to cover an X buys nothing and just adds a term, so it is never required.

items.prompt: Why do K-maps stop being practical past about 4 or 5 variables?
choices: The grid and its adjacencies get too large and error-prone to track by eye
Gray code only works for 4 variables
Boolean algebra forbids more than 5 variables
The output values become undefined
items.explanation: More variables mean exponentially more cells and harder-to-see adjacencies (a 5-variable map is two stacked 4-variable maps). Beyond that, people switch to tabular or automated methods.

items.prompt: What is the Quine-McCluskey method, in one line?
choices: A step-by-step table that finds the same prime implicants without drawing a map, easy for software to automate
A faster way to draw the K-map grid by hand
A method that only works for 2-variable functions
A way to add more variables to a truth table
items.explanation: Quine-McCluskey reaches the same prime implicants as a K-map using a systematic table instead of a picture, which is why it scales and why software tools use that style of approach.

items.prompt: A minimized circuit briefly glitches when an input change moves between two groups that only touch. The usual fix is to:
choices: Add one redundant term that bridges the two groups, smoothing the transition
Delete one of the two groups
Slow the whole circuit down permanently
Re-label the axes in plain binary
items.explanation: An extra (logically redundant) term covering the overlap keeps the output steady during the change, removing the glitch. It costs one gate but does not alter the truth table.

items.prompt: Does adding that redundant bridging term change what the circuit computes?
choices: No, the steady output is identical; it only affects timing during a transition
Yes, it flips several outputs from 1 to 0
Yes, it converts the circuit to product-of-sums
Yes, it removes an essential prime implicant
items.explanation: The redundant term is already implied by the others, so the final truth table is unchanged. Its only effect is to keep coverage continuous during an input change, killing the glitch.

items.prompt: A particular 1 can be covered by three different prime implicants. Is that 1's coverage forcing any essential prime implicant?
choices: No, because more than one prime implicant covers it, none of them is forced by that 1 alone
Yes, all three become essential
Yes, the largest of the three is essential
Yes, the smallest of the three is essential
items.explanation: An essential prime implicant is the ONLY one covering some 1. If three can cover this 1, you have a choice, so this 1 does not make any of them essential. Essentials come from 1s covered by exactly one prime implicant.
